coredata.dc:title: Tuning photodegradation performance using carbon quantum dots and niobium pentoxide
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of The editorial office of Journal of Materials Science & Technology.
coredata.prism:publisher: Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of The editorial office of Journal of Materials Science & Technology.
coredata.dc:creator: Spessato, Lucas
Crespo, Lucas H.S.
Silva, Marcela C.
Gibin, Mariana S.
Sato, Francielle
Winkler, Manuel E.G.
Almeida, Vitor C.
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQD) were employed as dopants to enhance the photocatalytic efficiency of Nb2O5 by decreasing the bandgap energy and prolonging the lifetime of the photogenerated exciton by increasing conductivity. X-ray diffraction (XRD), N2 porosimetry, scanning electron microscopy (SEM), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), photoacoustic spectroscopy (PAS), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS), zeta potential, and atomic force microscopy (AFM) were used to characterize the synthesized nanostructures. The residues from acerola processing were converted into CQD with an average size of 2.56 nm, as confirmed by AFM and the high fluorescence quantum yield of 43.32 %. N2 physisorption results showed that the CQD were deposited on the surface of Nb2O5, reducing the specific surface area (S BET) from 122±2.0 to 29±1.3 m² g–1. The photocatalytic performance of CQD/Nb2O5 was superior to that of the control materials under UV−vis light irradiation, as there was a decrease in the bandgap energy (E g) from 2.78 to 1.93 eV. This decrease in E g led to a significant increase in the apparent rate constant (k app) of the MG dye from 1.90 × 10–3 s–1 to 42.2 × 10–3 s–1, demonstrating that the presence of CQD can effectively separate the photogenerated charge carriers, as it was observed from the increase in conductivity showed by Nyquist diagram.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Acerola bagasse
Malachite green
Carbon dots
Photocatalyst
Chemical kinetics

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of CQD/Nb2O5 and bare Nb2O5

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: LS acknowledge Conselho Nacional de Desenvolvimento Científico e Tecnológico (CNPq) for his postdoctoral fellowship (Grant No. 168182/2022-0). VCS acknowledge CNPq (Grant No. 403525/2023-3 and 308314/2022-0) and Fundação Araucária (Grant No. JDT2022271000058) for his research project.

LS acknowledge Conselho Nacional de Desenvolvimento Científico e Tecnológico (CNPq) for his postdoctoral fellowship. VCS acknowledge Conselho Nacional de Desenvolvimento Científico e Tecnologico (CNPq–Brazil) for his research project (Grant No. 426890/2016-7 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Dynamic light scattering (DLS) size distribution of CQD.
AFM image and size distribution of CQD.
Absorption spectra from 30 to 120 mg L–1 of CQD (a) and photoluminescence emission spectra (b) of CQD.
XRD patterns of Nb2O5 and CQD/Nb2O5.
N2 physisorption isotherms for (a) Nb2O5, (b) CQD/Nb2O5, and (c) pore size distributions.
SEM images of Nb2O5 (a, b) and CQD/Nb2O5 (c, d).
Nyquist diagram collected at OCP, in PBS solution (pH 7.4), of the Nb2O5, CQD, and CQD/Nb2O5.
Photodegradation kinetic curves (25 °C, pH 6.0, catalyst dosage of 0.5 g L-1, and C 0 = 15 mg L–1).
Photodegradation kinetic curves with different scavengers (25 °C, pH 6.0, catalyst dosage 0.5 g L–1, C 0 = 15 mg L–1, and scavenger concentration = 20 mmol L–1).
Textural properties of Nb2O5 and CQD/Nb2O5.

head.ce:title: Tuning photodegradation performance using carbon quantum dots and niobium pentoxide

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, State University of Maringá, Av. Colombo 5790, Maringá, Paraná, Brazil
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aDepartment of Chemistry, State University of Maringá, Av. Colombo 5790, Maringá, Paraná, Brazil

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, State University of Maringá, Av. Colombo 5790, Maringá, Paraná, Brazil
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bDepartment of Physics, State University of Maringá, Av. Colombo 5790, Maringá, Paraná, Brazil

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Chemistry, University of Campinas, Campinas, CEP: 13083-970, SP, Brazil

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Chemistry, University of Campinas

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cInstitute of Chemistry, University of Campinas, Campinas, CEP: 13083-970, SP, Brazil

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQD) were employed as dopants to enhance the photocatalytic efficiency of Nb2O5 by decreasing the bandgap energy and prolonging the lifetime of the photogenerated exciton by increasing conductivity. X-ray diffraction (XRD), N2 porosimetry, scanning electron microscopy (SEM), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), photoacoustic spectroscopy (PAS), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS), zeta potential, and atomic force microscopy (AFM) were used to characterize the synthesized nanostructures. The residues from acerola processing were converted into CQD with an average size of 2.56 nm, as confirmed by AFM and the high fluorescence quantum yield of 43.32 %. N2 physisorption results showed that the CQD were deposited on the surface of Nb2O5, reducing the specific surface area (S BET) from 122±2.0 to 29±1.3 m² g–1. The photocatalytic performance of CQD/Nb2O5 was superior to that of the control materials under UV−vis light irradiation, as there was a decrease in the bandgap energy (E g) from 2.78 to 1.93 eV. This decrease in E g led to a significant increase in the apparent rate constant (k app) of the MG dye from 1.90 × 10–3 s–1 to 42.2 × 10–3 s–1, demonstrating that the presence of CQD can effectively separate the photogenerated charge carriers, as it was observed from the increase in conductivity showed by Nyquist diagram.

ce:section.ce:para: Malachite green (MG) is a water-soluble cationic dye, classified as triphenylmethane dye, which is widely used in textile and fish farming industries due to its low cost, ready availability, and efficacy against fish microbial pathogens [1]. It has been reported that MG is mutagenic, teratogenic, and carcinogenic, besides being particularly toxic to mammalian cells. Thus, MG has been banned by the US Food and Drug Administration from being used in the fish farming industry [1–3]. However, it is still widely used and the disposal of the effluents that contain inefficiently treated MG can cause environmental and public health problems.

To date, there is a countless number of methods capable of treating wastewaters that contain MG, such as adsorption [4], biodegradation [5], electrocatalytic degradation [6], Fenton-like processes [7], and heterogenous photodegradation [8,9]. Heterogeneous photodegradation is a promising technology, once it can mineralize pollutants by sunlight in the presence of a photocatalyst, such technique stands out among the others because the sunlight energy is inexhaustibly abundant, clean, and safe, providing non-hazardous and economically attractive processes [10].

Several semiconductors are used in heterogeneous photocatalytic degradation, such as TiO2, ZnS, CdS, FeS, PbS, reduced graphene oxide, quantum dots, and Nb2O5 [11–14]. Nb2O5 has been proven to be one of the greatest photocatalysts for several years of scientific investigation due to its chemical stability, nontoxicity, and singular photoelectrochemical properties in the presence of UV light [15–17]. The biggest problem related to photocatalysts is the high bandgap energy, causing them to be activated only under UV radiation (which encompasses ∼5 % of the solar spectrum), and not having a good performance under visible radiation [18]. To overcome these issues, scientists worldwide are producing semiconductors modified with carbon quantum dots (CQD) to boost the efficiency of photocatalytic degradation [19–24].

CQD are a new class of carbon-based nanomaterials under 10 nm with quasi-spherical morphology, high biocompatibility, the presence of modifiable surface functional groups, water dispersion, and distinctive photoluminescence and electron transfer properties [25]. Due to its desirable properties, CQD have become a compelling material for application in heterogenous photodegradation. Various studies have demonstrated the impact of CQD on semiconductor materials, Qu et al. [26] reduced the bandgap energy (E g) of KNbO3 from 3.30 eV to 3.25 eV using CQD, leading to a notable enhancement in the photodegradation of crystal violet dye from 41.5 % to 70.0 %. Dou et al. [27] modified CdS-TiO2 with CQD, resulting in a decrease in E g from 2.27 eV to 2.22 eV and a significant increase in the photodegradation of rhodamine B from 69.0 % to 84.5 %, accompanied by higher photocurrent density. Widiyandari et al. [28] doped ZnO with N-doped CQD, reducing the E g from 3.22 eV to 3.13 eV and significantly improving the methylene blue photodegradation from 10.0 % to 80.0 %. Wang et al. [29] utilized phosphorus-doped CQD on g-C3N4 to enhance the photodegradation of tetracycline hydrochloride, resulting in a decrease in E g from 2.80 eV to 2.74 eV, increased photocurrent density, and higher photodegradation efficiency from 18.0 % to 76.0 %. Nu et al. [30] noted that the presence of CQD reduces the E g of TiO2 from 3.2 eV to 2.91 eV. As a result, the composite exhibited superior photodegradation of methylene blue with 83.0 % efficiency compared to 27.0 % for pristine TiO2. These findings collectively demonstrate the ability of CQD to decrease E g and extend the lifetime of photogenerated excitons, resulting in enhanced photocatalytic processes.

A wide range of precursors that are typically considered residues can be used to produce economically attractive CQD, including orange peels [31], flower wastes [32], carbohydrates [33], and pollen [34]. Acerola bagasse is an excellent source for synthesizing CQD since it is rich in ascorbic acid and carbohydrates [35], which are labile organic compounds capable of starting chemical condensation to produce relatively small carbon structures. Brazil is the largest producer, exporter, and consumer of acerola fruits, with over 11,000 hectares of plantation and production of approximately 3000 kg/ha and 32,990 tons/year [36]. Acerola fruit is commercially processed into pulps and juices, leaving behind a dark red residue of low added value which is often used as fuel. By converting this underutilized material into a value-added product, CQD synthesis from acerola bagasse offers a sustainable and cost-effective approach for generating a functional material with potential application.

To the best of the authors' knowledge, there is no reported work on the utilization of acerola residue to synthesize CQD. As a result, the main aim of the present work is to produce CQD from acerola bagasse and use them as dopants in nanostructured Nb2O5, for further use as photocatalyst in heterogenous photocatalytic degradation of MG.

The acerola fruit was purchased from a local producer in Maringá-Paraná. Approximately 5 kg of the fruits were washed and crushed in batches with water using a conventional blender. The juice was then filtered with a 20-mesh sieve, and the filter cake was subjected to three crushing cycles to ensure high efficiency of fruit pulp extraction. The resulting cake from the final extraction cycle, known as acerola bagasse, was dried at 50 °C for 24 h and stored properly.

For the synthesis of CQD, 1.0 g of dried biomass was suspended in 45 mL of deionized water and 5 mL of concentrated hydrogen peroxide (H2O2 32 %). The resulting suspension was transferred to a hydrothermal reactor, made of stainless steel internally coated with Teflon®, properly sealed, and placed in an oven at 190 °C for 24 h. After natural cooling, the resulting solution was vacuum filtered with a cellulose membrane (Milipore® 0.45 µm) to remove small solids that were not degraded. After that, the filtered solution was dialyzed for 2 days using Spectra/Por® membrane with molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) from 100 to 500 Da. After that, the obtained inner solution from the dialysis bag was frozen with liquid N2 and lyophilized. The obtained solid CQD, after lyophilization, showed an average yield of 20 % ± 6 %. The solid CQD was redispersed in 250 mL of deionized water, reaching a concentration of approximately 0.8 g L–1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CQD/Nb2O5 and bare Nb2O5
ce:section.ce:para: The CQD/Nb2O5 material was synthesized according to the modified methodology of Leite et al. (2006) [37]. Initially, 8.0 g de niobium ammonium oxalate (NAO), with molecular formulae NH4[NbO(C2O4)2(H2O)2]·nH2O, provided by Companhia Brasileira de Metalurgia e Mineração (CBMM), were dissolved in 100 mL of a 60 mg L–1 CQD solution, and the mixture was magnetically mixed for 30 min until a clear solution was obtained. Then, hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), 32 % in volume, was added to a molar ratio 10H2O2:1Nb, instantly leading to a transparent yellow solution, indicating the formation of the niobium peroxo reactive complex. Finally, the resulting solution was transferred to hydrothermal reactors, sealed, and introduced in an oven at 120 °C for 12 h. After natural cooling, the synthesized yellowish solid material was centrifuged at 3000 r/min and purified with distilled water thrice. After purification, the material was dried at 50 °C for 48 h. The obtained mass of CQD/Nb2O5 was 1.20 ± 0.91 g. The theoretical mass fraction of CQD was approximately 0.50 %.
For the synthesis of bare Nb2O5 in the absence of CQD, the solution of 100 mL CQD 60 mg L–1 was replaced by 100 mL of deionized water.

The crystallographic analysis for CQD/Nb2O5 and Nb2O5 was carried out by powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) in the 2θ range from 5° up to 80° with a Shimadzu 7000 X-ray diffractometer, using a monochromatic radiation source of Cu K α (λ=0.1540598 nm) and resolution of 0.02° and acquisition time of 5 s. The average crystal size (e, nm) was estimated by the Debye-Scherrer equation (Eq. (1)), considering the most intense diffraction halo to determine the θ and β (full width at half maximum) values.
(1)
D =
0.9 λ
β cos θ

The textural properties of CQD/Nb2O5 and Nb2O5 were determined with N2 physisorption at –196 °C, using a surface area and pore size analyzer (QuantaChrome Nova 1200e). The specific surface area (S BET) was calculated by Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) equation from the linear relation between [n(1 −
p/p 0)] and the relative pressure range (p/p 0) from 0.05 to 0.30. The total pore volume (V T) was defined as the net amount of N2 adhered on the adsorbent surface at p/p 0=0.99, while the micropore (V µ) and mesopore (V M) volumes were estimated according to the α-plot and Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) methods, respectively. The pore size distribution was calculated using the non-local density functional theory (NLDFT), while the average pore size was obtained by the ratio 4S BET/V T. The morphologies of the materials were analyzed by scanning electronic microscopy (SEM) using an FEI Company-Quanta 250 with acceleration of 15 kV. The SEM images for CQDs were obtained after a lyophilization process.

The surface chemical compositions of CQD and CQD/Nb2O5 were investigated by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), using an ESCA-5800 spectrometer with a monochromatic Al Kα source (1486.6 eV). The survey spectra were collected in the binding energy (B.E.) range of 10–1100 eV with the pass energy of 187.85 eV. The high-resolution spectra of C 1s, N 1s, O 1s, and Nb 3d were deconvoluted after a Shirley background correction.

The zeta potential, ζ, and hydrodynamic sizes of CQD were determined in a Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZSP at room temperature. For ζ measurements, the material was dispersed in a 0.1 mol L−1 NaCl solution with pH from 3.0 to 11.0 (adjusted with HCl or NaOH solutions).

The optical properties of Nb2O5 and CQD/Nb2O5 were measured by photoacoustic spectroscopy (PAS). PAS equipment is composed of a 1000-W Xenon arc lamp (Oriel, model 68,820) used as a light source, which is diffracted when passing through the monochromator (Oriel, model 77,250). The monochromator was equipped with a diffraction grade (Oriel, model 77,296) for UV–Vis spectral range from 230 up to 800 nm. The nominal power of the light source was 800 W. The mechanical chopper (Stanford Research Systems, model SR 540) was tuned at 13 Hz modulating the light that impinges the sample. Thus, the beam was focused on the sample placed inside the photoacoustic cell, which was sealed with a transparent quartz window (8-mm diameter and 2-mm thickness). A Brüel and Kjaer model 2669 capacitive microphone was coupled to collect the photoacoustic signal that was generated from the pressure variation resulting from the periodic heating of the sample.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was performed in a conventional three electrodes cell configuration controlled by a 100 B electrochemical workstation (Gamry, USA). A platinum rod (d = 0.1 cm) was used as the counter electrode (CE), an Ag wire as a pseudo-reference electrode (d = 0.1 mm), and a glassy carbon (d = 0.0707 cm²) modified with Nb2O5, CQD, and CDQ/Nb2O5 as the working electrode. To perform the drop cast of the materials, a suspension was prepared at 10 mg mL−1 catalyst concentration in isopropanol and naphion-117 (4 % vol.). Then, an aliquot of each suspension was deposited on the glassy carbon to achieve 0.5 mg cm–2 loading. EIS was recorded at open circuit voltage (OCP) in the range of 20 kHz to 0.1 mHz with 10 mV as the DC bias voltage in 1X–PBS solution (pH = 7.4) as the electrolyte.

CQD UV–Vis spectra were obtained using an Agilent Cary 60 spectrophotometer with Δλ = 0.5 nm, scanning speed equal to 150 nm min–1, and spectral range from 200 to 500 nm. CQD fluorescence emission spectra were obtained using a Varian Cary Eclipse spectrofluorimeter. Fluorescence quantum yield was determined at the excitation wavelength (λ) of 325 nm according to Eq. (2).
(2)
Φ
f , CQD
=
Φ
f , s
I CQD
A s
η
CQD
2
I s
A s
η
s
2

In which
Φ
f
is the fluorescence yield, I is the integrated intensity of the emission spectrum, A is the absorption at λ, η is the solution refraction index (which was approximately to the water solvent, 1.33). The subscripts “CQD” and “s” refer to the carbon quantum dot material and standard solution, respectively. The chosen standard solution was a 0.1 mol L–1 fluorescein in PBS buffer (pH = 7.4). According to the IUPAC technical report,
Φ
f , s
for PBS buffered fluorescein is equal to 0.92 [38]. All solutions were N2 degassed before the fluorescence measurements to prevent O2 quenching.

The photocatalytic performance of all synthesized materials was evaluated using the experimental apparatus depicted in Fig. S1. The photoreactor employed in the study comprised a 500 mL jacketed beaker, measuring 11.0 cm in height and 8.5 cm in internal diameter. The system incorporated water recirculation at a temperature of 25 °C and a quartz tube housing the germicidal UV lamp (Oram, 18 W). The entire setup was placed on a magnetic stirrer to facilitate efficient mixing during the experiments.

For the photodegradation experiments, 400 mL of an MG dye solution (C 0 = 15 mg L–1 and pH = 6.0) were placed into the beaker, and 200 mg of the material under analysis was dispersed in this solution (dosage of 0.5 g L–1). Initially, the suspension was stirred in the dark for 1.0 h to ensure adsorption-desorption equilibrium. After this time, a 4-mL aliquot was withdrawn for analysis. Then, the germicidal lamp was turned on, and aliquots of the suspension were withdrawn. All aliquots were filtered using an Analytical® syringe filter (Nylon membrane, 13 mm filter diameter, and 0.22 µm pore diameter) pre-saturated with the initial MG dye solution. It is important to note that MG photolysis was performed following the same experimental procedure described above but with the absence of a photocatalyst. The absorbances of the solutions after irradiation were measured spectrophotometrically at the wavelength of maximum absorption (618 nm), and the concentration was calculated using the analytical curve (R 2 = 0.9992), within the linear range from 2.0 to 25.0 mg L–1.

Kinetic analyses often demonstrate that photocatalytic reactions on the surface of materials follow the Langmuir–Hinshelwood (LH) mechanism [39–42]. The mechanism postulates that the reaction rate is proportional to the surface coverage of the photocatalyst by the substrate. The simplest form of the LH mechanism is presented by the pseudo-first-order equation (Eq. (3)) [39–42], which is possible to determine the apparent rate constant (k app, min–1) by plotting C/C 0 vs t (min). The half-life time t 1/2 (min) can be determined from the expression t 1/ 2 = 0.693/k app.
(3)
C =
C 0
e
−
k app
t

The quantum efficiency of MG photodegradation,
Φ
P
, was calculated according to Eq. (4) [43].
(4)
Φ
P
=
k app
2 , 303
N em
ε
VM , λ
l
in which
k app
(s–1) is the apparent constant of MG photodegradation,
N em
is the number of photons emitted per second per illuminated volume of solution (1.41
×
10
− 6
Einstein L–1 s–1, λmáx=254 nm),
ε
VM , λ
is the molar absorption coefficient of MG at λ max of emission of the lamp (2703 L mol–1 cm–1), and l is the length of the photoreactor (11.0 cm).

The size distribution profiles of CQD by DLS as a function of concentration are presented in Fig. 1 . As can be observed, as the concentration of CQD suspension decreases from 400 to 30 mg L–1, the mean quantum dot size drops from about 190 nm to 28 nm, and the distributions become narrower. The results suggest that at high concentrations, the CQDs tend to aggregate more readily. Additionally, the hydrothermal treatment method demonstrated its ability to produce nanodispersed carbon dots with a minimum concentration of 60 mg L–1 required to achieve the smallest nanostructured size.

The zeta potential (ζ) results for the CQD as a function of pH are shown in Fig. S2(a). It can be observed that all ζ values are negative and range from –14.9 mV to –68.3 mV within the pH range of 3.0 to 11.0, with a material concentration of 30 mg L–1. Such observation indicates that more surface species of the material are being deprotonated, contributing to the enhanced stability of the suspended material. The negative ζ values are likely attributed to ionizable oxygenated groups, while the constant decrease of ζ values can be attributed to the hydrolysis of δ-lactonic groups (whose existence was proven by XPS results). This hydrolysis generates additional negative charges, leading to a decrease in ζ values.

The results of ζ as a function of material concentration (C 0) at fixed pH are presented in Fig. S2(b). It can be observed that ζ values increase towards neutrality, suggesting a growing instability within the colloidal system. The stability of colloidal systems, such as CQDs, is determined by the balance between attractive forces (specifically, London and van der Waals) and repulsive forces arising from electric double layers, according to the DLVO theory [44]. When repulsive forces overcome attractive ones, the suspension remains stable. However, it is important to mention that DLVO theory has limitations when describing high-concentration colloidal systems [45].

As CQD concentration increases, the proximity between nanoparticles monotonically decreases, leading to a stronger influence of complex non-DLVO attractive forces, which are oscillatory [45]. This attractiveness is highly dependent on the concentration of nanoparticles. Consequently, at high concentrations, CQDs tend to aggregate (as further verified by DLS measurements). This aggregation reduces the surface-volume ratio and, thus, leads ζ values to neutrality [45].

The AFM images and size distribution for the CQD are presented in Fig. 2 . As observed, the material indeed exhibits nanostructured characteristics. The statistical distribution showed that the average size of the material is 2.56 nm. This value differs from the one obtained using the DLS technique, as the latter employs the approach of hydrodynamic radius. Since the synthesized CQD possess a high hydrophilic nature, they are heavily solvated. Consequently, the apparent size of the material in water suspension is larger.

The SEM images of the CQD material, after freeze-drying, are shown in Fig. 3 . As can be observed, the hydrothermal treatment was able to produce a material with approximately spherical morphology on a microscopic scale. It is possible to see that the material also presents several cavities and pores, likely resulting from the absence of structural collapse mediated by the freeze-drying technique.

The optical properties of CQD have been studied using molecular absorption and fluorescence spectroscopies. The absorption spectra in different CQD concentrations (Fig. 4 (a)) show a band below 300 nm that corresponds to the π−π* transition of CC bond, and the tail between 300 and 400 nm can be attributed to the intrinsic absorption of the n–π* transition of CO bond [46]. The photoluminescence emission at an excitation wavelength of 325 nm as a function of CQD concentration is presented in Fig. 4(b). As observed, the intensity of photoluminescence decreases with increasing concentration. This phenomenon occurs due to the increased interactions between different polar groups at high concentrations which hampers the decay from the singlet first excited energy state (S 1) to the singlet ground energy state (S 0) [47]. The presence of a large amount of polar functionalities helps to form clusters at high concentrations, reducing the fluorescence intensity [48]. The digital images of CQD under white and black radiation (λ=365 nm) are shown in Fig. 4(b) (insert). As easily observed, the CQD exhibit a bright blue fluorescence after exposure to a wavelength capable of causing electronic excitation.

The fluorescence quantum yield of CQD was calculated based on the molecular absorption and fluorescence spectra. The absorbance values at a wavelength of 325 nm were 0.0853 and 0.0285 for CQD and the fluorescein standard, respectively, both in PBS buffer at pH 7.4 (Fig. S3). The integrated intensity values of the photoluminescence spectra were 67,658 and 47,894 for CQD and fluorescein standard, respectively, both in PBS buffer at pH 7.4. Therefore, the calculated
Φ
f , CQD
is equal to 43.35 %. The obtained result is equivalent to or higher than several other carbon dots obtained from biomass reported so far in Refs. [49,50]. It is important to emphasize that high
Φ
f
values lead to low photocatalytic activities, because the processes are competitive. For more details, check the photocatalytic performance section.

The X-ray diffractograms obtained for Nb2O5 and CQD/Nb2O5 are presented in Fig. 5 . As observed, the diffraction patterns are very similar between the materials, suggesting that they may consist of the same crystalline phases. The observed phase in both materials is attributed to orthorhombic Nb2O5, with the crystallographic planes (001), (101), (002), and (102) appearing at 2θ angles of 23°, 35°, 46°, and 55°, respectively. It is worth noting that the diffraction halos represented by asterisks (*) are attributed to the amorphous phase Nb2O5·nH2O, which is a consequence of the hydrothermal synthesis method used [14]. It is worth mentioning that, using the Debye-Scherrer equation for the crystallographic plane with the highest intensity (001), it was determined that the crystal sizes of Nb2O5 are approximately 12.8 nm, while CQD/Nb2O5 exhibited crystal sizes of approximately 10.9 nm.

The presence of CQD in the reaction medium induces a 15 % reduction in the crystal size of Nb2O5. This may be attributed to a potential correlation between the increased solubility of peroxo-niobium complexes upon the introduction of CQDs, thereby approaching a state of supersaturation. This observed effect could be a consequence of the high surface electric potential of CQDs. Once a high supersaturation degree is achieved, one triggers a simultaneous emergence of multiple precipitation nuclei, leading to the formation of multiple smaller crystals, as predicted by von Weimarn's Law [51–53].

The results of nitrogen (N2) physisorption at 77 K are shown in Fig. 6 . According to the IUPAC classification, the physisorption isotherm of Nb2O5 (Fig. 6(a)) resembles type IV(a), which is characteristic of predominantly mesoporous adsorbents commonly found in nanostructured oxides [54,55]. Furthermore, due to the gradual curvature of adsorption at low relative pressures, a similarity can be observed with the type I(b) adsorption branch, indicating the possible presence of a few micropores. Additionally, the hysteresis observed in the isotherm is attributed to type H2(b), which is associated with capillary condensation in mesopores [54]. On the other hand, the physisorption isotherm of the CQD/Nb2O5 (Fig. 6(b)) is similar to type II according to the IUPAC classification, which is attributed to non-porous or macroporous solids.

The results of the textural parameters are presented in Table 1 . As expected, the specific surface areas (S BET) for the synthesized materials are considerably different, with values of 122±2.0 m² g–1 and 29±1.3 m² g–1 for Nb2O5 and CQD/Nb2O5, respectively. This indicates that the CQD is deposited on the surface of the Nb2O5 material during the hydrothermal synthesis. Consequently, the pore analysis follows the same pattern. All pore volumes of the Nb2O5 material are larger than those of CQD/Nb2O5, with their values decreasing in the presence of CQD. It is important to note that low pore volume and SBET values are important to prevent significant pollutant adsorption on the surface of the material, as the development of a thick adsorbate layer would hinder the penetration of radiation to the photocatalyst surface, thus hindering exciton formation.

The pore size distributions of both Nb2O5 and CQD/Nb2O5 are shown in Fig. 6(c) As can be seen, the Nb2O5 material exhibits a narrow pore distribution from 3.34 to 12.11 nm, confirming its mesoporous structure [54]. Similarly, the CQD/Nb2O5 composite material also exhibits a mesoporous structure, as the pore diameters range from 3.18 to 29.4 nm. It is observed that the main difference between the pore distributions is the volume of adsorbed N2, indicating that the presence of CQD in the reaction medium was able to fill the mesopores of Nb2O5. It is important to note that all the porosity present in the materials is attributed to interparticle porosity, as it is of the same order of magnitude as the crystal size calculated from the XRD patterns [56].

The SEM images of the CQD/Nb2O5 and Nb2O5 materials are presented in Fig. 7 . It is evident that, during the formation of the Nb2O5 nanocrystals in the composite material the spherical morphology of CQD is completely lost.

The XPS measurements were carried out to evaluate the surface relative composition of all synthesized materials and the functional surface groups. Figs. S4 and S5 present the survey spectra, as expected the CQD did not present niobium on its surface. On the other hand, it was observed the presence of carbon (80.22 %) and oxygen (19.77 %). Acerola bagasse is made up of ascorbic acid, cellulose, and antioxidants (such as flavonoids), all these substances are potential carbon and oxygen sources, which justifies the high content of the elements on the surface of the material. Regarding CQD/Nb2O5, the elements C (23.65 %), O (49.11 %), N (12.64 %), and Nb (14.6 %) were detected on its surface. The presence of N is attributed to the Nb-precursor used in the synthesis (niobium ammonium oxalate), which has N in its molecular structure, helping to fix N on the surface of the material.

High resolution deconvoluted spectra of C 1s, O 1s, and Nb 3d are shown in Figs. S3 and S4 for CQD and CQD/Nb2O5, respectively. For the CQD, the C 1s spectrum showed the presence of three subspectra. The chemical shifts correspond to the carbon matrix (CC/CC) at 284.8 eV; phenolic, alcohols, ether, and amine groups (COC/CO/CN) at 286.0 eV; and carboxylic, ester and δ–lactonic groups (OCOR) at 288.5 eV [57]. For CQD/Nb2O5 the chemical shifts are similar, assigning for the same surface functional groups, except for the fact that the bond C–Nb at 283.6 eV is observed [58].

The oxygenated groups identified in the C 1s spectra are confirmed in the O 1s spectra. As regards O 1s high-resolution spectra, two subspectra were identified for CQD, referring to the groups CO (530.8 eV) and COC/COH (532 eV) [57]. Three subspectra were identified for CQD/Nb2O5, characteristics of CO and (NbO)lattice at 531.9 eV, (NbO)surface at 530.5 eV, and (O2–)lattice at 528.9 eV. It is important to point out that the presence of O2– lattice on CQD/Nb2O5 can lead to the increase in photogenerated radicals compared to CQD [59]. As for Nb 3d spectrum, the subspectra at 207.4 eV and 210.1 eV are indexed to the energy states Nb 3d5/2 and Nb 3d3/2, respectively, characteristic to the Nb5+existence in Nb2O5 [60].

The results of photoacoustic spectroscopy in the UV–Vis region are presented in Fig. 8 (a). As observed, the Nb2O5 and CQD/Nb2O5 exhibit significant differences in their absorption profiles. The Nb2O5 material shows an absorption range from 450 to 230 nm, while the CQD/Nb2O5 material has an absorption range from 600 to 230 nm. These absorption regions are closely related to electronic transitions from the valence band to the conduction band. It is easy to notice that the CQD/Nb2O5 exhibits a bathochromic shift compared to the control material, indicating that the CQDs obtained from acerola bagasse act as doping agents in the Nb2O5 structure, contributing to a reduction in the bandgap.

The Tauc equation (Eq. (5)) was fitted to the results of photoacoustic spectroscopy to calculate the bandgap energies of the CQD/Nb2O5 and Nb2O5 samples.
(5)
(
α h ν
)
1 n
= A
(
h ν −
E g
)
where α is the absorption coefficient, ν is the frequency of vibration, h is the Planck constant, E g is the bandgap value, A is the proportional constant, and n denotes the nature of the sample transition. It is known that Nb2O5 follows an indirect allowed transition, n = 2 [61,62], e.g., without electron angular momentum conservation. The value of E g can be determined from the plot of (αhν)1/² vs hν, where the intercept on the abscissa is approximately equal to the value of E g.

The E g values found for CQD/Nb2O5 and Nb2O5 are 1.93 eV and 2.78 eV, respectively (Fig. 8(b)). Such values demonstrate that both materials exhibit semiconductor characteristics [14,63]. Furthermore, the presence of CQD in the reaction medium was able to cause a decrease in the value of E g for Nb2O5. Based on the results, it can be recognized that the absorption energy responsible for exciting the electrons from the valence band to the conduction band occurs at 642 nm for CQD/Nb2O5 and 446 nm for Nb2O5. Therefore, the irradiation from the chosen lamp is capable of photogenerate excitons.

In addition to the band gap information of the materials, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy was used for the evaluation of the intrinsic capacitances and resistances. The Nyquist diagram is presented in Fig. 9 , it is possible to notice that Nb2O5 presented the highest value in both real (Z') and imaginary ( −
Z'') impedance axes, e.g., showed the widest semi-circle. This implies higher capacitance and resistance values than the others. For CQD/Nb2O5, the smallest half circle was observed and, therefore, it showed the lowest capacitance and resistance. These results confirm the improved electronic conductivity of CQD/Nb2O5, which exhibited the lowest charge transfer resistance [64–66].

The photocatalytic efficiencies of the synthesized materials were evaluated through photodegradation assays of MG, quantifying the dye concentration as a function of the exposure time to UV–vis radiation. The photodegradation and photolysis experiments, as well as the adjustment of the kinetic equation, are presented in Fig. 10 .

As observed, photolysis of MG dye revealed that approximately 14 % of the initial concentration was photodegraded after 4 h of irradiation. In contrast, when exposed to the same experimental conditions, MG exhibited photodegradation percentages of 36.7 % with Nb2O5, 64.2 % with CQD, and 94.3 % with CQD/Nb2O5. These variations in photodegradation can be attributed to differences in the external quantum efficiency (EQE) [67]. EQE is a well-established concept in the scientific literature, commonly expressed as the product of several key elementary processes, including light absorption leading to the formation of excited electron-hole pairs, subsequent charge separation, and the transfer of these charges to surface redox pairs [67].

It is seen that the composite CQD/Nb2O5 exhibits the highest photodegradation activity among the evaluated materials, this evidence can be explained based on the findings of the characterization results. The enhanced photoactivity can be attributed to the lowest band gap energy value, measured at 1.93 eV, which facilitated efficient light absorption and the production of excitons. Furthermore, the improved surface electronic conductivity plays a critical role in promoting the separation of charge carriers and their subsequent transfer to surface redox pairs. This phenomenon facilitates the production of free radicals, which are essential for the degradation of MG molecules in the photodegradation process

The pseudo-first order kinetic model was fitted to the experimental data, the parameters k app, t 1/2, and the overall quantum yield of photodegradation (
Φ
P
) are shown in Table 2 . It is possible to observe that the presence of a photocatalyst increases the k app and reduces t 1/2. In the absence of a photocatalyst, t 1/2 is equal to 1326 min, while in the presence of Nb2O5, CQD, and CQD/Nb2O5 the t 1/2 values are 364, 142, and 16 min, respectively. The CQD/Nb2O5 showed the highest k app (42.2
×
10
− 3
min–1) and the lowest t 1/2 (16 min).

The photocatalytic quantum efficiency of CQD is lower than the CQD/Nb2O5 probably because of its high fluorescence quantum efficiency (43.35 %). According to the Jablonski diagram, the processes of fluorescence decay and radical production are competitive [47]. If the fluorescence quantum efficiency is high, then there is a lower probability for the first excited singlet electronic states (S 1) to undergo intersystem crossing to the excited triplet state (T 1), which is responsible for producing free radicals [47].

The understanding of the photodegradation mechanism of MG was achieved through the application of different experimental conditions, varying the initial pH (ranging from 3.0 to 8.0), initial MG concentration (ranging from 5.0 to 15 mg L–1), and photocatalyst dosage (ranging from 0.25 to 1.0 g L–1). The results are shown in Figs. S6–S8.

The observed similarity in the photokinetic curves across a wide range of experimental conditions serves as compelling evidence of the robust efficiency exhibited by the CQD/Nb2O5 photocatalyst. However, it is possible to observe the apparent rate constant slightly changes as a function of the experimental conditions—the values can be seen in Table S1. It is seen that as the MG concentration increases, there is an associated decrease in the rate constant. This behavior is likely attributed to the reduced capacity of light to penetrate the catalyst surface within a high-concentration environment or the insufficiency of photogenerated radicals available for the initial-time degradation of all MG molecules [68]. Varying the dosage of CQD/Nb2O5 has no significant effect on MG photodegradation until 0.5 g L–1. Beyond this critical dosage, there is a slight rate constant increase, likely attributed to the enhanced photon absorption due to the higher catalyst amount, leading to greater exciton generation and, consequently, a rate increase constant [69]—the continuous catalyst dosage increase tends to reduce the rate constant due to increased turbidity, hindering light penetration.

From Table S1 it is also possible to observe that as the solution pH increases, the rate constant rises. This is due to the greater availability of hydroxyl ions (OH –) at higher pH levels, facilitating the formation of •OH. Such radical is the predominant species at higher pH, and its production is enhanced by oxidizing an increased number of OH– on the catalyst surface. Consequently, this pH-dependent mechanism ultimately enhances the process efficiency [70].

The photodegradation mechanism of MG was elucidated through the application of different radical scavengers, such as benzoic acid (BA), ascorbic acid (AA), potassium iodine (KI), and oxalic acid (OA). It is published that BA is a hydroxyl radical (•OH) scavenger, AA is an efficient superoxide radical (
O 2
• −
)
scavenger, and both KI and OA are active holes in the valence band (
h
VB
+
) scavengers [71,72,59,73,74]. The photodegradation results in the presence of the scavengers are shown in Fig. 11 . The most decrease in the photodegradation efficiency is attributed to the BA presence, confirming the vital role of •OH for MG degradation. Furthermore,
O 2
• −
is also important to aid the MG photodegradation, once the efficiency is greatly decreased in the presence of AA. It is interesting to note that the photodegradation kinetic curves in the presence of KI and OA scavengers are very similar to the control experiment (absence of scavengers), evidencing that photogenerated
h
VB
+
are not the main ones responsible for direct or indirect MG molecule oxidation. Overall, the results suggest that MG degradation is governed by •OH and
O 2
• −
radicals produced from electrons on the conducting band (
e
CB
−
)
.

The photodegradation rate constant values of several CQD-based photocatalysts used for the degradation of MG are presented in Table S2. Upon analysis, it is evident that CQD/Nb2O5 prepared in this manuscript stands out as one of the most effective photocatalysts among those documented in the literature.

The residues from acerola processing were successfully converted into carbon quantum dots (CQD) with an average size of 2.56 nm and a fluorescence quantum yield of 43.35 %. The CQD material, composed of carbon and oxygen, exhibited monodispersity in water at concentrations below 60 mg L–1 with zeta potential ranging from –14.9 mV to –68.3 mV in the pH range from 3.0 to 11.0. Furthermore, the incorporation of CQD on nanostructured Nb2O5 resulted in the formation of CQD/Nb2O5, which demonstrated a reduced band gap energy (E g) compared to the control material (Nb2O5), decreasing from 2.78 to 1.93 eV. The photocatalytic degradation of malachite green (MG) was evaluated using the obtained materials, CQD/Nb2O5 exhibited the highest degradation apparent rate constant (k app) of 42.2 × 10–3 min–1 and the shortest half-life time (t 1/2) of 16 min. The superior photodegradation efficiency of CQD/Nb2O5 can be attributed to the synergism between its lower band gap energy and the improved surface electronic conductivity observed from the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: LS acknowledge Conselho Nacional de Desenvolvimento Científico e Tecnológico (CNPq) for his postdoctoral fellowship (Grant No. 168182/2022-0). VCS acknowledge CNPq (Grant No. 403525/2023-3 and 308314/2022-0) and Fundação Araucária (Grant No. JDT2022271000058) for his research project.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.jmst.2023.12.039.
